data.title: Trump invokes Cold War-era Defense Production Act to fund new energy projects
data.description:
President Donald Trump invoked the Defense ​Production Act to sign ‌a series of presidential memorandums providing federal funds for a wide range of energy projects, the White House announced Monday, as his administration faces pressure to help curb rising oil, gasoline, and electricity costs.

Trump signed five memorandums under the law, targeting coal supply chains, ​domestic petroleum production, natural gas transmission and ​liquefied natural gas capacity, and power grid infrastructure - areas where the president said insufficiencies threaten national defense.

Projects eligible for support could include coal-fired power plants, refineries and facilities that manufacture gas turbines and transformers - equipment that has been subject to shortages.

The move allows the Department of Energy to deploy funding that was secured last year in Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill tax-and-spending package.

The Defense Production ​Act is ​a ⁠Cold War-era piece of legislation aimed at boosting production of goods ​for national security purposes.

On Monday, crude oil futures recovered more than half of Friday's losses as uncertainty over the prospect of peace talks between the U.S. and Iran and the status of the Strait of Hormuz re-ignited supply fears as the end of a two-week ceasefire approaches.

"The sudden sense of optimism that overwhelmed markets on Friday has given way to threats and accusations," Mizuho's Robert Yawger said in a note. "Meanwhile, the Strait of Hormuz remains shut, and every day the strategic choke point remains closed brings the world one day closer to an energy crisis."

Iranian state media cast doubt on the likelihood of a second round of talks, saying the U.S. must lift its blockade on Iranian ports; after-hours, crude oil fell following reports indicating that Iran would send a team to Pakistan for talks.

Oil prices could rise to $110/bbl if traffic in the Strait of Hormuz remains disrupted for another month, Citigroup analysts said.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery rallied 6.9% to $89.61/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery jumped 5.6% to $95.48/bbl, the largest one-day gain for both benchmarks since April 2.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) edged higher for a fourth straight session, helped by a slight cooling in temperature outlooks; the front-month Nymex May contract settled up 0.6% at $2.689/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: ServisFirst projects 7-9 bps margin expansion while building Texas opportunity into the "B" range
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ServisFirst Bancshares (SFBS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We're really pleased with our start to the year" and "we had pretty solid loan growth for the quarter," while "loan payoffs begin to diminish compared to the last 2 years," said Chairman, President & CEO Thomas Broughton.
  • Broughton highlighted pipeline strength and Texas expansion: "the forward loan pipeline over 90 days... is the strongest we've ever had in our history" and "We actually closed our first loan in Texas," adding "we do have 18 bankers on board there today."
  • On funding and operating performance, Broughton said, "On the deposit side, they grew by 8% annualized in the first quarter" and "our efficiency ratio continues to be the best-in-class as we dropped below 30% in the first quarter," while also noting hiring: "We do have 161 producers at quarter end" and "we've hired over the last 12 months, 32 new FTEs."
  • "For the first quarter of 2026, we reported net income of $83 million or $1.52 per diluted share or $1.54 on a normalized basis," said Executive VP & CFO David Sparacio. "The net interest margin expanded to 3.53%... and 61 basis points better than the same quarter last year," and "we redeemed the $30 million and 4.5% subordinated notes due in November of 2027."

Outlook

  • "I expect the margin to expand 7 to 9 basis points given a flat rate environment," Sparacio said, while adding uncertainty: "nobody obviously knows what the Fed is going to do with rates" and "If they do reduce rates once, we're going to aggressively drop our rates as well on deposits."
  • Sparacio framed the repricing tailwind: "For the next 12 months, we have about a $2 billion opportunity for low fixed rate loans renewing" and "we have about $2.9 billion in fixed rate loans maturing in the next 3 years." He added: "we have $1.2 billion in loan maturities... in the next 12 months" with "weighted average yield... 5.19%" and "Our going on rate for new loan activity is 6.5%."
  • On expense trajectory, Sparacio said, "I think if you're using that 30% mark, we're not going to dip too much lower" and "I would say mid- to high single digits" for expense growth.

Financial Results

  • Sparacio said Q1 results included "net interest income... $148.2 million" and "noninterest income... $10.8 million," while the linked-quarter fee line comparison reflected that "fourth quarter included a $4.3 million nonrecurring BOLI death benefit" and Q1 had "a prior period adjustment to BOLI income of $1 million."
  • On fees and expenses, Sparacio reported "Service charges were $3.3 million," "Mortgage banking revenue was $1.9 million," and "Net credit card income... $2.2 million," while "noninterest expense... $47.4 million" and "the efficiency ratio came in at 29.81%."
  • On tax and capital, Sparacio said, "Our effective tax rate for first quarter was 17.83%" reflecting "the purchase of investment tax credits," and capital metrics included "Common equity Tier 1... reached 11.86%" and "tangible common equity to total tangible assets stood at 10.46%." He also noted liquidity: "$1.84 billion in cash" and "We have no FHLB advances. We have no broker deposits."

Q&A

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: "how much have you seen out of the New Texas team..."; Chairman, President & CEO Broughton: "they have a robust pipeline" and "towards the end of the year, we think we'll certainly see some success in closing," while cautioning, "there's a fair amount of price and credit term competition that we try not to take part in."
  • Scouten, Piper Sandler: "do you think maybe that average earning asset growth can more closely match the growth in loans..."; Executive VP & CFO Sparacio: "I would expect average assets to rise in line with loan growth."
  • Scouten, Piper Sandler: expense run-rate question; Sparacio: "I would advise you not to use the $4.4 million number" and "I think it's closer to a $5.5 million number," citing "a $1.2 million benefit" from "a reduction in the special assessment from the FDIC."
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: efficiency ratio durability and expense growth; Sparacio: "dropping below 30%, I think, is kind of a flattening point" and "I would say mid- to high single digits."
  • Moss, Raymond James: margin and repricing pickup; Sparacio: "I expect the margin to expand 7 to 9 basis points" and "their weighted average yield is 5.19% today" vs. "6.5%" for new loans.
  • Moss, Raymond James: large borrower status; Senior VP & Chief Credit Officer Jim Harper: "None of our borrowers to date have filed bankruptcy" and "slow and steady is probably the way I'd characterize it"; Broughton: "We think we'll see good progress in the next 2 quarters, [6] months."
  • David Bishop, Hovde Group: Texas scale; Broughton: "I would think it would be more like a [ B instead of an M ]" over "3- to 4-year" and Harper added, "It's virtually all C&I at this point."
  • Bishop, Hovde Group: loan payoff trend; Broughton: "It's trended down. It's more like $0.30" and "probably 20%, 25% of bookings" rather than "the 50% payoff."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts appeared slightly positive and detail-oriented, emphasizing modeling items (expense run-rate, margin bridge, Texas ramp), including Scouten’s focus on translating trends into growth and Moss pressing on "how much additional margin expansion you expect."
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks (Sparacio: "a strong start to 2026 across virtually every metric we track") and more cautious on competitive dynamics and macro uncertainty in Q&A (Broughton: "there's a fair amount of price and credit term competition" and "the wildcard... is... gas prices").
  • Versus last quarter, management messaging stayed confident on operating discipline and repricing, while Q1 added more specificity on Texas execution ("18 bankers") and credit visibility (Harper: "confident in some near-term reductions in NPAs").

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Texas buildout accelerated from "9 members on the Houston team" in Q4 2025 to "18 bankers on board" in Q1 2026, alongside Q1’s update that the team "leased" a "26,000 square feet" office and "closed our first loan in Texas."
  • Repricing narrative remained consistent, shifting from Q4’s "about a $2 billion opportunity" (including cash flows and modifications) to Q1’s framing of "about a $2 billion opportunity" over the next 12 months and "about $2.9 billion" maturing over three years.
  • On credit, Q1 disclosed "net charge-offs... around $8.3 million" and signaled near-term NPA relief ("reductions... of approximately $17 million"), while Q4 emphasized the year-over-year NPA increase was "associated with exposure to a single merchant developer."
  • Efficiency ratio expectations shifted: Q4 suggested Texas could be "a drag" toward "between 30% and 33%" in 2026, while Q1 described sub-30% as a "flattening point" and said it would not dip much lower.

Risks and Concerns

  • Broughton flagged macro sensitivity: "the wildcard on everything with the consumer is... gas prices" and said it could "trickle into the whole economy."
  • Management emphasized competitive pressure: "a fair amount of price and credit term competition" and that ServisFirst may lose deals when competitors accept lower returns.
  • On asset quality, Harper cited elevated NPAs ("Nonperforming assets to total assets... were 100 basis points") but pointed to actions: "confident in some near-term reductions in NPAs of approximately $17 million" tied to specific payoffs/assumptions.
  • On the large borrower, Harper said, "we're continuing to proactively work with the borrower," and Broughton added the timeline expectation: "good progress in the next 2 quarters, [6] months."

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized improving balance-sheet momentum (solid loan growth, an historically strong 90+ day pipeline, and 8% annualized deposit growth) alongside continued margin tailwinds from repricing low fixed-rate loans. The Texas expansion was positioned as a meaningful multi-year growth lever, with headcount doubling to 18 bankers and an initial loan closed, while leadership reiterated discipline on pricing and expenses, projecting modest further margin expansion and mid- to high single-digit expense growth amid competition, rate uncertainty, and ongoing work on a large borrower and NPA reductions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bank of Hawaii targets ~2.9% NIM by year-end 2026 while lowering overhead growth outlook to 2.5%-3%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bank of Hawaii (BOH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Bank of Hawaii delivered another solid set of results to open 2026." (President, CEO & Director James Polk) "Net interest income and our net interest margin expanded for the eighth consecutive quarter... NIM increased 13 basis points... We remain on track toward our stated goal of approaching 2.9% NIM by the end of the year."

  • "Normalizing for nonrecurring expenses and noninterest income, our EPS came in at $1.39." (President, CEO & Director Polk) "We maintained strong capital and excellent credit quality while continuing to build on our leading deposit market share position here in Hawaii."

  • "We are in the early stages of assessing the potential impact of Typhoon Sinlaku, and it will take several weeks to gain clearer insight." (President, CEO & Director Polk) "This overlay accounts for the potential impact of flood damage to approximately 15 to 20 properties in our portfolio, net of anticipated insurance recoveries." (Vice Chair & Chief Risk Officer S. Shairson)

  • "For the quarter, we reported net income of $57.4 million and a diluted EPS of $1.30." (Vice Chair & CFO Bradley Satenberg) "These declines were primarily the result of elevated noninterest expense... including... $2.8 million... payroll taxes and benefits... [and] a nonrecurring compensation-related charge... $3.5 million... [and] an unrelated severance charge of $750,000."

Outlook

  • "At the moment, we are currently forecasting no rate cuts in 2026." (Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg) "While I still anticipate that we will see some modest improvements in our cost of deposits going forward, any material changes will likely be contingent upon future Fed rate adjustments."

  • "My expectation is that the second quarter noninterest income will be approximately $42 million." (Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg) "Second quarter normalized noninterest expense is expected to be approximately $112 million."

  • "As a result, I'm lowering my forecasted range for annual growth in overhead expenses to between 2.5% and 3% or 0.5% lower than my previous forecast." (Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg)

  • "I am currently planning to repurchase an additional $15 million to $20 million of stock during the second quarter." (Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg)

Financial Results

  • "NIM increased 13 basis points" and management tied the quarter’s margin expansion to repricing and funding costs: "we remixed $643 million in fixed rate loans and investments from a roll-off yield of approximately 4% to a roll-on yield of 5.6%." (President, CEO & Director Polk)

  • "Despite two fewer days this quarter, NII grew by $5.6 million." (Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg) "Our cost of deposits declined to 1.26%, representing a 17 basis point reduction as compared to the linked quarter."

  • "Noninterest expense was $116.1 million compared to $109.5 million during the linked quarter." (Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg) "Going forward, I expect that this [FDIC insurance] assessment will be approximately $3.2 million or $0.5 million less per quarter than our recent run rate."

  • Credit performance was described as unusually strong: "Net charge-offs totaled $1.1 million or just 3 basis points annualized... 3 basis points is abnormally low." (Vice Chair & Chief Risk Officer Shairson)

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: asked whether the full-year expense growth view excludes certain items; Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg: "That's inclusive of that. So we're saying $112 million, all inclusive of every expense that we're aware of today."

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: asked about consumer-loan momentum and potential upside to growth; President, CEO & Director Polk: "until we get better clarity in the overall environment, we're still... in that low single-digit growth outlook," while citing initiatives including "direct mailing" for home equity and "digital contracting" to "speed up funding time frame" in indirect.

  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens Inc., Research Division: asked about deposit competition and CD repricing; Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg: "The competitive landscape is, it's reasonable and it's rational, and we still think there's an opportunity to continue to reprice our CD book," with renewals "expected to renew at rates ranging from 2.25% to 3%."

  • Kelly Motta, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: asked about proposed capital rule changes; Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg: "we have done some early assessments of the impact, and it will be positive for us... a 50 to 100 basis point improvement."

  • Jared David Shaw, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about tourism demand risk; President, CEO & Director Polk: "it's probably too early to tell," and management flagged monitoring "energy prices... airfares and its ultimate impact on tourism" amid geopolitical uncertainty.

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: challenged loan growth framing; President, CEO & Director Polk: "It was for the overall portfolio" and "we're really comfortable in that low to mid-single-digit number" because "greater uncertainty" increased.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on durability of loan growth, deposit competitiveness, capital impacts of proposed rules, and the cadence of NIM expansion.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A, including "we are watching the environment carefully" (President, CEO & Director Polk) and "it's probably too early to tell" on tourism impacts (President, CEO & Director Polk).

  • Versus last quarter’s more uniformly upbeat framing ("We recorded yet another set of strong results" (Chairman & CEO Peter Ho)), the current call added more explicit uncertainty around geopolitics, storms, and the rate path, while still reiterating the year-end NIM goal.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership changed, with "this is my first earnings call as CEO" (President, CEO & Director Polk) following prior-quarter remarks led by "Chairman & CEO" Peter Ho.

  • Margin trajectory language stayed consistent, with both calls pointing to an exit-rate target near year-end 2026: "approaching 2.9% NIM by the end of the year" (President, CEO & Director Polk) vs. "NIM by the end of 2026 could come in near the 2.90% range" (Chairman & CEO Ho).

  • Capital return became more explicit: Q1 included "planning to repurchase an additional $15 million to $20 million" in Q2 (Vice Chair & CFO Satenberg) versus Q4’s "anticipate that we'll be closer to the $15 million to $20 million range moving forward per quarter" (Chairman & CEO Ho).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Tensions in the Middle East, rising energy costs and the potential for sustained inflation are headwinds that could affect consumer confidence and travel demand." (President, CEO & Director Polk)

  • "This ACL coverage does include a $3.2 million qualitative overlay specifically related to the recent Kona Low storm." (Vice Chair & Chief Risk Officer Shairson) "We are in the early stages of assessing the potential impact of Typhoon Sinlaku." (President, CEO & Director Polk)

  • On loan growth, management tied outlook conservatism to uncertainty: "we're going to need a little more certainty in the environment" (President, CEO & Director Polk) and the company cited the "situation in Iran" as a driver of caution. (President, CEO & Director Polk)

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized continued NIM expansion driven by fixed-asset repricing and easing deposit costs, reiterating a goal of approaching a 2.9% NIM exit rate by year-end 2026 while forecasting no rate cuts in 2026. The quarter reflected higher seasonal and nonrecurring expenses, but the company lowered its full-year overhead growth outlook and laid out an active buyback plan for Q2. In Q&A, executives repeatedly linked loan-growth conservatism and tourism monitoring to heightened uncertainty, while highlighting strong credit metrics and storm-related reserving actions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chevron says weekend fire at Pasadena, Texas, refinery now extinguished
data.description:
A Saturday fire that shut down a reformer unit at Chevron's (CVX) 125K bbl/day Pasadena, Texas, refinery has been extinguished with no injuries reported, according to Dow Jones' OPIS, citing a company spokesman and regulatory filing.

The refinery experienced a leak and fire in a process unit that was quickly extinguished with no injuries reported, the company said.

The filing with the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality identified a reformer at the process unit, which was shut down after the fire, adding that the emissions event lasted for just over one hour.

The Pasadena refinery, located ~10 miles southeast of downtown Houston, has an estimated 24K bbl/day of catalytic reforming capacity; it produces gasoline, gasoline components, diesel, fuel gas, and liquefied petroleum gas, and processes sweet, light crude that Chevron (CVX) produces from the Permian Basin.

data.title: Environmentalists sue to stop BP ultra-deepwater project in U.S. Gulf
data.description:
Environmentalists have sued to reverse the Trump administration’s approval of BP's (BP) $5B Kaskida ultra-deepwater drilling project in the Gulf of Mexico, alleging the proposed project threatens the region's ecosystems and endangered species.

Groups including the Center for Biological Diversity, Sierra Club, and Healthy Gulf said in the lawsuit filed with the 11th Circuit U.S. Court of Appeals that the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management approved a plan with faulty estimates for a worst-case oil spill from the project, which is expected to produce 80K bbl/day of oil.

BP's (BP) proposal lacked required information and does not demonstrate that it can safely drill as far as six miles below the sea floor, the lawsuit said, noting the drilling depth for the Kaskida project will be greater than that of Deepwater Horizon, which exploded in 2010 and led to the biggest oil spill in U.S. history.

BP (BP) said the lawsuit was "unfounded" and that it “singles out BP in an apparent broader effort to block not only the Kaskida project but all future offshore oil and gas development in the U.S."

The Kaskida platform represents "a major step forward," the U.S. Interior Department said, unlocking more than 275M barrels of previously unrecoverable oil in the Gulf.

data.title: Uber discloses 11.52% stake in Lucid
data.description:
Uber Technologies (UBER) disclosed an 11.52% passive stake in Lucid (LCID), representing over 37.75M shares.

The shares are held through SMB Holding, an indirect, wholly owned subsidiary of Uber, according to a regulatory filing.

Shares of Newark, California-based Lucid Group were up over 1% in extended trading on Monday.

Earlier on April 14, Lucid priced a $300M public offering of common stock, part of a larger $1.05B capital raise that includes investments from Uber and Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund.

As part of the pact, Uber (UBER) said it will increase its purchase commitment to at least 35,000 Lucid vehicles designed exclusively for use as part of Uber's future global robotaxi service. Uber has also committed to an additional $200M investment in Lucid, raising Uber's total investments to $500M to date.

data.title: Gold and silver slide on renewed Middle East war tensions
data.description:
Gold and silver futures fell Monday as ​the dollar and oil ​prices rose following Iran's threat of retaliation in response to the seizure of an Iranian cargo vessel by U.S. forces, dimming hopes of a breakthrough in efforts to end the Middle East war.

Iran wavered on whether to send diplomats to Pakistan for a second round of peace talks after the U.S. seized the ship and maintained its blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

The U.S. dollar rose to its highest level in a week before paring gains to trade up ‌0.1%, and benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury yields gained, increasing ​the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding bullion.

"The latest weakness has been driven by renewed dollar strength and fresh concerns about energy-led inflation," Saxo Bank analysts said in a note. "Gold and silver remain highly sensitive to developments in the Middle East given the knock-on impact on the dollar, bond yields, and U.S. rate expectations."

Meanwhile, gold demand during one of India's key buying festivals remained subdued on Sunday as record prices ​curbed jewelry purchases, offsetting a ‌modest uptick in investment demand.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery closed 1% lower at $4,806.60/oz, and front-month Comex April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) settled down 2.2% to $79.951/oz.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (DUST), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Snap appoints insider Doug Hott as new CFO
data.description:
Snap (SNAP) said it has appointed insider Doug Hott as its new Chief Financial Officer.

The social media company’s current CFO, Derek Andersen, will depart, effective May 8, 2026, according to an SEC filing.

Hott, 53, is the current Vice President of Finance, Strategy, and Corporate Development in the company since July 2024. Before that, Hott served as Snap’s Vice President of Finance.

The company's stock was trading 0.8% lower after-hours on Monday.
